mnemonics: I.E.I. - Interrupts are Enabled by Setting I=1, Disabled by Setting I=0
Master Switch: Remember the Global Interrupt Enable (GIE) bit is like a master power switch for all maskable interrupts.
Priority Order: Use the acronym 'NMI' for Non-Maskable Interrupts, remembering they 'Never Mind' being disabled.
keyPoints: Interrupts are typically enabled/disabled by setting or clearing a specific bit in a control register (e.g., the I-bit in a processor status register).
Disabling interrupts is a critical method for achieving atomic operations and preventing race conditions during critical sections of code.
Some interrupts, like Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMIs), cannot be disabled by software as they are reserved for critical, high-priority events.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to re-enable interrupts after a critical section, which can cause the system to become unresponsive to important events.
Assuming that disabling interrupts protects a shared resource in a multi-core system; this only works on a single core as each core has its own interrupt enable flag.
Attempting to disable a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) through software, as its enabling is typically handled by hardware design.
